coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of an ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensor coupled with biofunctionalized zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots for rapid detection of cephalexin

Roberts, Akanksha
Gandhi, Sonu
coredata.dc:description: The unregulated usage of Cephalexin (CFX) in animal source food products has led to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in humans. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials possessing both unique optical and electrical propertiesbased on their tuneable size that serves as an excellent signal enhancer. The fluorescence quenching and conductive properties of GQD were exploited for the detection of CFX. In this study, a zero-length conjugation approach was utilized to develop Cephalexin-Bovine Serum Albumin (CFX-BSA) conjugate and used to develop antibodies (Ab). Conjugated CFX-BSA Abs with GQD enhanced the electrochemical response of the sensor for sensitive detection of CFX. The fabricated electrode was optimised by Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). The limit of detection for CFX was found to be 0.53 fM in standard buffer with negligible cross-reactivity against other β-lactam antibiotics. The biofunctionalized electrode based on GQD-antibody may potentially be miniaturised for on-site detection of other antibiotics in food samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of CFX-BSA conjugates: a) Steps involved in synthesis of CFX-BSA conjugates which include addition of i. EDC, ii. NHS and iii. BSA; b i) TLC of activation of CFX via EDC-NHS method, shows distinct separated spots of activated CFX (C) and un-activated CFX (B) as compared to inactivated CFX (A); ii) TLC of CFX-BSA conjugate (E) in comparison with pure BSA (D); c) FT-IR of activated (act) CFX (orange line) and inactivated CFX (red line); d) FT-IR of CFX-BSA conjugate (green line) in comparison with pure BSA (blue line).

Characterization of CFX-BSA conjugate: a) UV–vis spectra of various ratios of CFX-BSA compared with pure BSA protein; b) Fluorescence spectra of different ratios of CFX-BSA compared with pure BSA protein; c) MALDI-TOF result showing molecular weight and no. of CFX molecules attached for BSA and CFX-BSA conjugates at different molar ratios; d) Native PAGE gel (a → g: BSA → 1:200) showing shift in band at 1:200 M ratio conjugate in comparison to control BSA.

Characterization of CFX antibodies: a) Serum ELISA after each booster with CFX-BSA conjugate and BSA; b) SDS PAGE gel where 1 and 2 signify the protein ladder and purified CFX-Ab respectively; c) Binding ELISA of purified Ab with CFX-BSA and only BSA protein; d) Competitive ELISA of CFX and other β-lactam antibiotics against CFX antibody.

Optimization and characterization of GQD and CFX-Ab for bioconjugation: a) Fluorescence spectra of GQD with decreasing fluorescence as the concentration of GQD decreases (1–0.2 mg/ml); b) Fluorescence quenching effect upon conjugation of Ab onto GQD; c) FT-IR spectra showed functional group changes during conjugation of GQD with Ab; d) TEM images displayed i) bare GQD; ii) Ab coated GQD.

DPV analysis of CFX on the fabricated GQD/CFX-Ab electrode: a) DPV measurement of different CFX concentrations (1 µM–1 fM) on fabricated electrode; b) Calibration curvebetween log of the various CFX concentration (*No error bars at certain points represent negligible deviation in the readings); Detection of CFX in spiked food samples (egg, meat and milk) in comparison with other β-lactams and cephalosporines. Cross-reactive analysis showed high specificity towards CFX than other cross-reactive species when tested in c) buffer; d) spiked egg samples; e) spiked meat samples; f) spiked milk samples; g) Repetitive DPV response of the same electrode which shows drop in signal after 5th round; h) Stable response of electrode over the period of 28 days showed very little change in output.
Summary and comparison of currently available electrochemical sensors for CFX detection.

row.entry: Electrochemical Sensor Cephalexin cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)

head.ce:title: Fabrication of an ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensor coupled with biofunctionalized zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots for rapid detection of cephalexin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: DBT-National Institute of Animal Biotechnology (NIAB), Hyderabad 500032, Telangana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: DBT-National Institute of Animal Biotechnology (NIAB), Hyderabad 500032, Telangana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of an electrochemical immunosensor using graphene quantum dot as a signal enhancer for ultrasensitive detection of cephalexin in animal-based food products.

The unregulated usage of Cephalexin (CFX) in animal source food products has led to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in humans. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials possessing both unique optical and electrical propertiesbased on their tuneable size that serves as an excellent signal enhancer. The fluorescence quenching and conductive properties of GQD were exploited for the detection of CFX. In this study, a zero-length conjugation approach was utilized to develop Cephalexin-Bovine Serum Albumin (CFX-BSA) conjugate and used to develop antibodies (Ab). Conjugated CFX-BSA Abs with GQD enhanced the electrochemical response of the sensor for sensitive detection of CFX. The fabricated electrode was optimised by Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). The limit of detection for CFX was found to be 0.53 fM in standard buffer with negligible cross-reactivity against other β-lactam antibiotics. The biofunctionalized electrode based on GQD-antibody may potentially be miniaturised for on-site detection of other antibiotics in food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Cephalexin (CFX) is a semi-synthetic cephalosporin antibiotic that belongs to the group of β-lactam antibiotics and is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that works against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. The mode of action of CFX is bactericidal, as they interfere in synthesis of the bacterial cell walls by inactivating penicillin-binding proteins and inhibiting the cross-linking of peptidoglycans (Castle, 2007). CFX is commonly used in veterinary medicine to prevent and cure mastitis (Lata et al., 2016) and other infectious illnesses in farm animals. Additionally, they promote growth and nutrition efficiency while also synchronizing the reproductive cycle and breeding performance. Consistent use of antibiotics, results in residue deposition in animal products such as meat, milk, and eggs which are consumed by humans. The build-up of these residue deposits poses numerous health risks (Boisseau, 1993) along with the development of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). To protect human health, these residues in animal-based foods must be regulated and as a safety measure, the European Union has set the Maximum Residue Limit (MRL) value of CFX at 100 µg/kg.

Traditional methods for the detection of CFX in foods is carried out by microbiological assays or β-lactam receptor-dependent assays (Lamar & Petz, 2007), instrumental methods such as liquid chromatography (Liu et al., 2011; Oliveira et al., 2007) or liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (W. Li et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018), or electrophoretic techniques such as capillary electrophoresis (Flurer, 1997) or capillary zone electrophoresis (Shen et al., 2007). Although well developed, these traditional methods are relatively costly, laborious, and require well-maintained facilities with special expertise to operate the system. Recently, techniques such as immunoassays (Chen et al., 2009) or sensor-based detection (Banga et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2017; Nandi et al., 2020) have begun captivating attention due to rapid and robust detection of analytes. The introduction of electrochemical sensors for diagnostics is a recent breakthrough in the field of diagnostics (Beitollahi, Tajik, Dourandish, et al., 2020; Beitollahi, Tajik, Reza Aflatoonian, et al., 2020; Jahani et al., 2020; Mahari & Gandhi, 2022b; Mohamadzadeh Jahani et al., 2020; Org et al., 2020; Roberts & Gandhi, 2022; Tajik et al., 2021; Tajik, Beitollahi, Mohammadi, et al., 2020; Tajik, Beitollahi, Nejad, et al., 2020). The advantage of using electrochemical based sensors is higher sensitivity, low sample volume required and no effect of real sample turbidity on results (Dey et al., 2022; Islam, Shukla, Bajpai, Han, Huh, Ghosh, et al., 2019; Islam, Shukla, Bajpai, Han, Huh, Kumar, et al., 2019; Mahari et al., 2022; Mahari & Gandhi, 2022a; Roberts, Kesarwani, Gupta, & Gandhi, 2022; Roberts, Mahari, & Gandhi, 2022; Shahdeo, Roberts, et al., 2022). Different types of nanomaterials have been employed for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes for e.g., graphene (Narlawar & Gandhi, 2021; Shahdeo et al., 2020), iron oxide (Chouhan, Horvat, et al., 2021), and gold nanoparticles (Talan et al., 2018). Nanomaterials display great value for sensing and fabrication of new sensors with improvised functioning (Bajpai et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2021). Carbonaceous materials have currently been garnering attention in the area of nano sensing and are being used to fabricate electrodes for electrochemical detection because of their unique properties such as non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and ease of synthesis (Chouhan, Jerman, et al., 2021; Roberts et al., 2019, 2020; Shahdeo, Chauhan, Majumdar, Ghosh, & Gandhi, 2022; Shukla et al., 2020). Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are 0 dimensional nanomaterials useful for their unique features like signal amplification, high biocompatibility, fluorescence, customizable particle size, electro-catalytic activity, and simultaneous and multiple biomolecule detection. Furthermore, their photo-stability against both photo-bleaching and blinking, as well as their ruggedness, inertness, non-toxicity, long-term stability, and water-solubility makes them an excellent alternative for inorganic structures (Mansuriya & Altintas, 2020). While GQDs retain the characteristic properties of graphene, it has the added advantage of quantum confinement size and edge plane site effects, resulting in opening the electronic bandgap which attributes to a sufficiently larger surface to volume ratio which increases electrochemical sensitivity for various nanoscale electronic applications (Gupta et al., 2017). Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) electrodes have potential applications in electrochemical sensing due to lower resistance, narrow bandgap than regular semiconductors, increased transparency, and higher electron mobility (Ahammad et al., 2018).

In this work, we have made use of the electrochemical property of graphene quantum dots which were localized onto FTO electrodes for robust detection of CFX. While the CFX molecule by itself is too small to illicit an immune response, it can act as an immunogen when it is covalently attached to a protein using a hapten determining factor attached to the ε-amino group of the lysine amino acid (Lima et al., 2020). CFX was conjugated with Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) using carbodiimide chemistry and was thoroughly characterized by chromatographic and spectroscopic techniques such as Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC), UV–Visible spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FT-IR) spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy, Polyacrylamide Gel Electrophoresis (PAGE) and Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization-Time of Flight (MALDI-TOF). The conjugate was further used for generation of antibodies against CFX which were analysed via Enzyme Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) to check for specificity and cross-reactivity. Electrochemical detection of CFX was performed on FTO using GQD-Ab modified electrodes. Different parameters were standardized such as frequency, pH of buffer, temperature, and response time using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The limit of detection (LOD) of the fabricated immunosensor for detection of CFX was found to be 0.53 fM using Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV). The biosensor showed repeatable results up to 5 times and could be stored for up to 28 days at 4 °C, with a response time of 30 s and further used to detect CFX in spiked food products. Compared to all existing sensors developed for cephalexin detection, the immunosensor developed in this work is highly sensitive and since both the CFX-BSA conjugate for immunisation and polyclonal antibody receptor components were developed in-house, the overall cost of fabrication was reduced. Furthermore, the use of fluorescence as well as electrochemical properties of zero-dimensional GQD for cephalexin sensing has been reported for the first time in this research work. Hence, we conclude that the fabricated electrode could successfully be used for ultrasensitive detection of CFX in spiked milk, egg and meat samples.

All the reagents and chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade unless mentioned otherwise. Cephalexin, Cefixime, Cefadroxil, Cefaclor, Penicillin, Ampicillin, Sodium dihydrogen phosphate-1-hydrate (Na2HPO4·H2O) were acquired from Merck (India). Sodium carbonate anhydrous (Na2CO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate (KH2PO4), sodium citrate tribasic dehydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O), 2-(N-morpholino) ethane Sulfonic Acid (MES), graphene oxide (GO), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), l-ascorbic acid, potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) and potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6·3H2O) were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (India). Sodium chloride (NaCl) was obtained from CDH (New Delhi, India). 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) Carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), bovine serum albumin (BSA), complete Freund’s adjuvant (CFA), incomplete Freund’s adjuvant (IFA), Tween-20, Skimmed milk powder, Graphene Quantum dots (GQD), FTO electrodes (10 mm × 30 mm), Thin Layer Chromatographic Plate (TLC Plate) and goat anti-rabbit IgG were procured from Sigma Aldrich (India). Protein-A Sepharose resin was bought from Cytiva (Marlborough, USA). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Hi-media (India). Polystyrene ELISA plates were acquired from NUNC (Denmark). A New Zealand white rabbit (7 weeks) was procured from Vyas Labs (Hyderabad, India) and housed at the small animal facility managed by the National institute of Animal Biotechnology (NIAB). All solutions were prepared in double distilled water unless stated otherwise.

Systonic S-924 Single-Beam UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Delhi, India) was used for UV spectral analysis, while Infra-red spectra were performed on Thermo Scientific-Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform-Infrared (FT-IR) Spectroscope (Bangalore, India). Fluorescence analysis was performed on PerkinElmer Enspire multimode Plate reader. Immunoassay plates were read on ELISA reader from Thermo-Fisher Scientific MultiskanTM FC Microplate Photometer (Bangalore, India). Electrophoretic experiments were performed on BIORAD Mini-PROTEAN Tetra Vertical Electrophoresis Cell (Gurgaon, India), while gel images were captured using Thermo-Fisher Scientific iBright™ CL1500 Imaging System (Bangalore, India). Conjugation of antibody on GQD was visualised under 200 kV JEOL JEM-2100 Transmission Electron Microscope (Tokyo, Japan) using Ted Pella Inc. copper TEM grids (Redding, California, USA). EIS and DPV measurements were performed with PalmSens4 (Netherlands). All experiments were performed at room temperature (RT- 25 °C) and repeated thrice unless stated otherwise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CFX hapten
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of CFX hapten, zero length conjugation method was applied. CFX solution was prepared in 2-(N-morpholino) ethane sulfonic acid (MES) buffer (pH 6.5) having concentration 100 mM, mixed with equimolar concentration of EDC and incubated for 2 hr. pH of the solution was maintained for reactivity of EDC and all the reagents were added at 4 °C, while the activation of CFX was monitored by TLC. After 2 hr incubation, equimolar concentration of NHS was added. Activated CFX solution was further conjugated with BSA. The prepared conjugate was mixed vigorously followed by incubation for 14 hr at 4 °C, and different molar ratios of the conjugate were prepared (1:20, 1:40, 1:60, 1:80, 1:100, and 1:200) by varying the concentration of activated CFX. Unreactive species were removed by buffer exchange dialysis using PBS, and the conjugate synthesis was confirmed by TLC by comparing with BSA as a control. CFX-BSA conjugates were also analysed by UV–vis spectroscopy by comparing absorbance against BSA solution at 260 nm (Kachab et al., 1992). Functional group analysis of the conjugate was confirmed by FT-IR analysis. The effect on the change in mass of BSA upon conjugation was observed on Native Polyacrylamide Gel-Electrophoresis (PAGE) and MALDI-ToF. After complete characterization, the selected hapten was stored at 4 °C for further experimentation.

CFX-BSA hapten conjugate was further used for the generation of antibodies against CFX. 7-week-old female New Zealand white rabbit was initially subcutaneously immunized with emulsion prepared using Freund’s complete adjuvant and hapten (1:1 ratio), where the final concentration of the hapten was 200 µg. Furthermore, the animal was immunized with 150 µg of conjugate with Freund’s incomplete adjuvant as booster doses every 21 days till the titer value of serum antibody dropped. After the 5th booster, serum titer value reduced and hence the animal experiment was terminated by injecting barbiturates intravenously. Blood was collected from the animal, serum separated after each booster and the titer value of serum antibody was determined via serum binding ELISA on 96-well microtiter plates coated with 1.25 µg/ml CFX-BSA and 1:10,000 serum dilution. The serum was then purified by affinity chromatography by passing through a gravity flow column packed with Protein – A Sepharose resin following the catalogue protocol (Protein A-Sepharose® CL-4B Cytiva). Eluted fractions of antibody were collected and dialyzed against PBS. The purity of the antibody was verified using SDS-PAGE and stored at −20 °C with 0.01 % sodium azide as a preservative.

Binding ELISA experiments were performed with CFX-BSA on 96 well ELISA plates coated with 1.25 µg/ml CFX-BSA (in carbonate buffer, pH 9.6) followed by overnight (O/N) incubation at 4 °C. To remove excess unbound conjugate, the plate was washed methodically with 0.02 % PBS-T (Tween-20 in PBS). To block unbound sites, the plate was incubated for 1 hr with 2 % PBS-M (skimmed milk in PBS). After incubation followed by triple washing, primary Ab was incubated for 2 hrs at 37 °C in concentrations ranging from 5 µg/ml to 2.45 ng/ml. Further, the plate was incubated for 1 hr after addition of anti-rabbit anti-IgG-HRP secondary Ab solution followed by thorough washing. Horse-Radish Peroxidase (HRP) reactive substrate, TMB was added and kept in the dark for 15 mins at room temperature (RT). To stop the reaction, 1 N HCl was added and the colour intensity of the reaction was measured at 450 nm.

To check the specificity and reactivity of antibodies toward CFX and other cross-reactive antibiotics, competitive ELISA was carried out using the concentrations of coating conjugate and Ab standardised from the above binding ELISA. 96 well ELISA plates were coated with 1.25 µg/ml CFX-BSA (in carbonate buffer, pH 9.6) followed by O/N incubation at 4 °C, washing and blocking. Different concentrations of CFX (varying from 10 µg/ml to 6 pg/ml) were made separately in 0.1 % PBSM buffer and mixed with primary Ab concentration of 2.5 µg/ml, and added to the plate followed by incubation for 2 hr. Remaining steps and reagents were the same as binding ELISA and the same experiments were carried out by replacing the antibiotic with cross reactive species in the primary Ab step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofunctionalization of GQD with CFX-Ab and its characterization
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs have the ability to carried out redox reactions, help in charge transfer, and π-π interactions with the analyte (Ramezani et al., 2019). The conjugation of CFX-BSA antibody with GQD by carbodiimide chemistry (East et al., 2014) was carried out by activating the GQD surface with EDC-NHS in equimolar ratio. Different concentrations of GQD and antibody required for the optimum conjugation were analyzed via fluorescence spectroscopy by studying the fluorescence and quenching effect respectively. Further change in functional groups upon conjugation were confirmed using FT-IR and fluorescence spectroscopy. Finally, bare GQD and GQD-Ab conjugates was visualized under a transmission electron microscope at 200 kV by drop casting the samples onto carbon mesh coated TEM copper grids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accretion and optimization of FTO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: 100 µl of activated GQD and CFX-Ab was fabricated onto the FTO electrode. The optimum concentration of GQD and Ab required for electrode fabrication was standardized by checking fluorescence intensity and quenching properties of various concentrations. FT-IR analysis was also carried out to confirm the change in functional groups upon conjugation of GQD and CFX-Ab. For electrochemical detection, Ag/AgCl was used as areference electrode, platinum was used as a counter electrode and fabricated (GQD/Ab) electrode was used as a working electrode. For impedimetric measurement, instrument was set to scan frequency ranging from 0.01-106 Hz A.C potential set at 0.01 V. Various parameters such as frequency (8–32 Hz), response time (1–150 s), pH (6.0–8.0), and temperature (RT, 37, 4, 30) were optimised and maximum signal strength was reported. Variable CFX antigen (Ag) solution within the range of 1 fM to 1 µM prepared in 50 mM of Phosphate Buffer (PB) and detected using the fabricated electrode and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated. Furthermore, specificity of the modified electrode was also tested against 1 µM of cross-reactive antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CFX and other cross-reactive antibiotics in spiked food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed immunosensor was tested on antibiotic-spiked animal source food products such as milk, egg, and meat to negate the matrix effect. Milk sample was prepared by addition of 36 N acetic acid in milk for defatting, and the fat separated by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The meat sample was macerated in PB and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min before collecting the supernatant. The egg sample was prepared by homogenizing both egg yolk and egg white and mixed with PB in 1:1 ratio. Before detection all samples were spiked with 1 µM of CFX. 100 µl of spiked food samples were added to the buffer in the cell containing the electrodes. Specificity of electrode towards the analyte was observed by spiking 1 µM of cross-reactive species such as penicillin, ampicillin, amoxicillin, cefaclor, cefixime, and cefadroxil in buffer as well as spiked food samples. Repeatability and stability of developed electrode was also tested over 6 cycles and by storing at 4 °C for a period of 4 weeks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of synthesised CFX-BSA conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a explains the carbodiimide chemistry applied for the conjugation of CFX and BSA where EDC (Fig. 1a i.) enabled the activation of the carboxyl group present in CFX, at ideal pH range 5–6 in MES buffer. EDC converted the less-reactive carboxyl group into unstable O-acyl-urea derivative which then reacted with the amine group of NHS (Fig. 1a ii.) to form NHS-ester of CFX which is more stable than the O-acyl-derivative. This NHS ester was then allowed to react with the primary amine group present on BSA protein (Fig. 1a iii.) and form an amide bond between CFX and BSA at physiological pH. In Fig. 1bi, the activation reaction of CFX was characterized by TLC where two distinct spots of the activated CFX (C: Rf = 0.84) and un-activated CFX (B: Rf = 0.56) in the EDC-NHS reaction sample were observed against control CFX (A: Rf = 0.56). Furthermore, a change in the Rf value was observed in Fig. 1bii between BSA (D: Rf = 0.86) the CFX-BSA conjugate (E: Rf = 0.96). The conjugation was further confirmed by the differences observed in the FT-IR spectra peaks due to change in bonding between the atoms and functional groups which was seen in Fig. 1c, where the red line corresponding with pure CFX, showed a medium peak at 1730 cm−1 of carboxylic group of acid while the orange line corresponding to activated CFX showed a broad carboxylic peak of amide at 1680 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 peak of –NO along with –CO bending peak of ester at 1210 cm−1 present in NHS-ester derivative of CFX. In Fig. 1d, in pure BSA (blue line), 1° amide peak was observed which was also present in CFX-BSA conjugate (green line) due to formation of amide bond between CFX and BSA at 1690 and 1630 cm−1 respectively which confirmed the conjugation of CFX-BSA.

The optimum molar concentration required for synthesis of the conjugates was confirmed by UV–vis spectrum of different molar ratio conjugates which showed extent of CFX binding to the BSA molecule. In Fig. 2 a, BSA protein showed UV absorption (mainly due to aromatic residue present on it tryptophane, tyrosine, phenyl alanine) at 280 nm (Samari et al., 2012), and a blue shift from 280 nm to 262 nm corresponding to addition of CFX groups onto the protein molecule (Kachab et al., 1992). Extent of conjugation is directly proportional to intensity of absorbance and 1:200 M ratio conjugate showed maximum signal intensity. A similar but reverse effect was observed on the relative fluorescence of the CFX-BSA conjugates in Fig. 2b, as the addition of CFX molecules quenched the internal fluorescence of BSA compared to the native protein and maximum quenching was observed at 1:200 M ratio conjugate. Also, the conjugation of CFX onto the protein increased the molecular mass of the protein, as the number of molecules attached to the protein increased which was observed in MALDI analysis (Fig. 2c) where 1:200 M ratio conjugate showed highest molar size and maximum number of CFX molecules attached to the BSA protein. Finally, on the Native PAGE gel (a → g: BSA → 1:200), a shift in the band was observed in the conjugate in comparison to pure BSA (Fig. 2d) which supported the inference from the above characterizations of different molar ratio conjugates and hence, 1:200 M ratio was selected as the optimum hapten ratio for further antibody generation as it reflected 9 molecules of CFX per BSA in MALDI analysis which presumably helped in better antibody response.

On the basis of affinity chromatography, antibodies were separated from collected serum after each booster dose and serum ELISA was carried out to confirm the presence of CFX antibodies before antibody purification. The serum after each booster showed slightly higher affinity for CFX-BSA than pure BSA as observed in Fig. 3 a. The purity of IgG antibody was confirmed by SDS-PAGE, where the denatured antibody after complete gel run showed two distinct bands corresponding to the heavy chain having molecular weight approximately 50 kDa and light chain having molecular weight approximately 25 kDa (Fig. 3b).

From binding ELISA of CFX-BSA conjugate and generated Ab (Fig. 3c), the binding was confirmed and optimum concentrations of conjugate and Ab were standardized as 1.25 μg/ml and 2.5 μg/ml respectively for competitive ELISA. Specificity of generated antibody was determined by competitive ELISA (Fig. 3d) against both specific and non-specific antigen which include cephalosporine group antibiotics showing analogous behavior with cefalexin such as Cefadroxil, Cefaclor, Cefixime, Amoxicillin, and Penicillin. The Limit of Detection (LOD) was determined as 19.5 ng/ml and IC50 was calculated as 127.05 ng/ml against cefalexin with negligible cross-reactivity against the other β-lactam antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accretion and optimization of FTO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Variable concentration of GQD were analysed for fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4 a) and the optimum GQD concentration was fixed at 0.6 mg/ml for all the further experimentation as the intensity did not increase substantially beyond that concentration due to saturation. Similarly, from Fig. 4b, the Ab concentration were determined by keeping the fixed GQD concentration from above experiment and Ab concentration was varied from 0.2 µg to 1 µg and the maximum quenching effect was observed at 0.4 µg which was selected as the optimum concentration. Furthermore, the conjugation of antibody to GQD was confirmed by FT-IR, where in the conjugated GQD sample, a prominent peak of carboxylic group (amide) at 1650 cm−1 with sharp bending peak of amine at 1250 cm−1 and -CN stretch at 1490 cm−1 was observed, while in the unconjugated GQD sample, only a prominent peak of carboxylic group was observed at 1710 cm−1 (Fig. 4c). From the TEM images, monodispersed, individual bare GQDs were observed (Fig. 4di) whereas after conjugation, a proteinaceous layer of Ab can be seen deposited around the GQDs (Fig. 4dii) resulting in increased size.

In fabricated GQD/CFX-Ab electrode, GQD served as both an electrochemical catalyst as well as increased the surface area to support the Ab immobilized onto the electrode. Since resistance is applicable in DC potential circuit while impedance is analogous to resistance in AC measurement, AC was applied in the impedance experiments and a change in the impedance was observed. The value of the diameter of the EIS semicircle is known as the charge transfer resistance (RCT) and the lower the RCT (lower impedance), higher is the electrocatalytic activity towards the electrode i.e. higher the current output (Yu et al., 2000). In Figure S1a, it was observed that the blank or bare electrode showed the highest impedance to the applied signal frequency and addition of GQD decreased the impedance drastically attributable to the highly conductive nature of GQD which increased current and decreased RCT which is similar to DC and resistance correlation. Addition of CFX-Ab layer on the surface of GQD decreased the conductive behavior of the electrode by blocking the diffusion of redox species and hence the RCT increased. Moreover, as the Ag i.e., CFX was added to electrolyte solution, as CFX itself is an electroactive species and undergoes electrochemical oxidation, the conductive nature and charge transfer of the electrode was elevated which led to a change in capacitance, ultimately causing a decrease in the RCT. Optimum response time for binding of Ab to the target Ag was observed by comparing the generated signals at different time intervals from 5 to 150 s and it was observed that highest RCT was after 30 s (Figure S1b). EIS signals generated by applying AC voltage across a range of frequencies (8–80 Hz) showed the highest RCT at 80 Hz (Figure S1c) which was selected for best results. In electrochemical detection, temperature is an affecting factor as it changes the electrolyte properties like ionic movement and viscosity and therefore during optimization of electrode, the temperature for analysis was also standardized. Electrode was tested at different temperatures to check the impedance response, and the highest RCT was observed at room temperature (Figure S1d). Working pH of the electrolyte solution for the optimum impedance of the system, was determined against a pH range of low-neutral-high pH and from Figure S1e, it was observed that at slightly alkaline pH i.e., pH 7.5, was ideal for maximum RCT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical behavior of fabricated electrode and detection of cephalexin spiked real samples
ce:section.ce:para: CFX as an analyte was detected by Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV) with scan rate 0.1 V/s and against variable range of Ag concentration from 1 µM to 1 fM using the developed FTO electrode modified with GQD/CFX-Ab. From Fig. 5 a, the increase in peak current due to increase in electrochemical oxidation caused by addition of CFX in the redox buffer was observed (Li & Chen, 1993; Ojani et al., 2010).

The graph in Fig. 5b shows the linear regression equation acquired from the DPV result of CFX-Ab against CFX Ag. The value of intercept is 1.425 ± 0.01656 and slope is 0.04270 ± 0.003102 with R2 value of 0.9595. The efficiency in detection of working electrode i.e., the limit of detection was found to be 0.53 fM using the formula 3(Sy/S), where Sy corresponds to the standard deviation of the response and S corresponds to the slope of the calibration curve with the signal/noise ratio = 3. The fabricated immunosensor for CFX detects concentration in the femtomolar range, and the sensitivity of the present electrode is much higher as compared to other immunosensors for CFX listed in Table 1 .

The fabricated FTO/GQD/CFX-Ab electrodes were also checked for specificity and cross reactivity with other β-lactam antibiotics in redox buffer wherein the concentration for all cross-reactive species was kept at 1 µM. The differential pulse response of the electrode was found to be very similar to blank for penicillin, ampicillin, amoxicillin, cefaclor, cefixime, and cefadroxil whereas it was nearly 1.5 times elevated when tested against specific CFX antigen (Fig. 5c). Furthermore, to negate the matrix effect caused due to other components in real samples, the fabricated electrode was tested for detection of β-lactam and cephalosporines in spiked food samples such as egg (Fig. 5d), meat (Fig. 5e), and milk (Fig. 5f). In all cases it was observed that the non-spiked sample and cross-reactive antibiotics such as penicillin, ampicillin, amoxicillin cefaclor, cefixime, cefadroxil showed similar low peak currents whereas the samples spiked with CFX showed an increase in peak current which shows specificity of the developed sensor towards CFX in spiked food samples. Repeatability of the electrode is determined by the generation of similar current output over repetitive use, and it was observed that the fabricated electrode showed good repetitive results up to the 5th trial (Fig. 5g) with standard deviation of 498.0598 µA, after which a substantial decrease in the current was observed. Fig. 5h shows stability of the electrode tested over intervals of 7 days (day 1, 7, 14, 21, 28) with only a slight decrease in current signal on the 7th day, which demonstrated that the electrode could be used for testing samples in a laboratory set up when stored at 4 °C for 4 weeks with no effect in activity since even after a month, the signal strength of electrode showed efficient working.

In the current work, with the help of zero-length conjugation, CFX-BSA conjugate was synthesized, thoroughly characterized and further used as a hapten for generation of antibodies. The generated CFX-antibody was purified and characterized using SDS-PAGE and ELISA with the detection limit determined as 19.5 ng/ml and IC50 calculated as 127.05 ng/ml. Furthermore, an electrochemical sensor using the antibody as a bio-recognizer was developed by drop casting GQD coupled with Ab onto an FTO surface. The FTO/GQD/CFX-Ab electrodes were used to detect CFX content in buffer as well as spiked food samples and showed a limit of detection of 0.53 fM along with minimal cross reactivity against other similar antibiotics. The fabricated immunosensor was stable up to 28 days and showed a steady response up to 5 times of repeated use. Hence, the simulated immunosensor has the capacity to be developed into a small-scale, swift and highly sensitive detection tool for CFX and other β-lactam antibiotics in food samples.

Pratik Kolhe: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Akanksha Roberts: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sonu Gandhi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.
